title: ggml : add PolarQuant KV cache quantization (pq4_0, pq3_0, pq2_0)
body:
## Summary

Adds three new quantization types for KV cache compression based on PolarQuant (Zandieh et al., 2025) — a polar coordinate decomposition approach that eliminates per-block scale/zero-point overhead at extreme bit widths.

- **`pq4_0`** — 4-bit PolarQuant angles, 2.06 bpw, no residual correction
- **`pq3_0`** — 3-bit PolarQuant angles + 1-bit QJL sign correction, 2.56 bpw
- **`pq2_0`** — 2-bit PolarQuant angles + 1-bit QJL sign correction, 2.06 bpw

All three are accessible via `--cache-type-k` / `--cache-type-v`.

## Motivation

Current KV cache quantization at 4-bit (`q4_0`) stores a 16-bit scale per 32-element block — 25% overhead at that depth, worse at lower bit widths. PolarQuant avoids this entirely:

1. **Walsh-Hadamard rotation** spreads energy uniformly across dimensions (O(n log n), in-place, deterministic — no storage cost)
2. **Polar coordinate pairing** converts adjacent elements to (radius, angle) — angles concentrate after rotation, enabling uniform quantization without per-block calibration
3. **QJL sign-bit correction** stores the sign of the quantization residual (1 bit/element) and applies a principled correction during dequant, scaled by the analytically-derived RMS quantization error for the chosen bit depth

The result is ~2-bit KV cache with a single `ggml_half` radius per 256-element block as the only metadata.

## How it works

**Quantize (from_float):**
```
input[256] → FWHT → pair(x[2i], x[2i+1]) → atan2 → quantize_angle → pack
                                            → sqrtf → block_norm → d (ggml_half)
                                            (pq3/pq2: compute residual sign bits → qj[])
```

**Dequantize (to_float):**
```
unpack angles → cos/sin lookup table → Cartesian pairs
    → (pq3/pq2: apply QJL sign correction)
    → inverse FWHT → output[256]
```

Key implementation details:
- Sin/cos reconstruction uses precomputed lookup tables (16/8/4 entries) — no runtime trig on the dequant path
- Block size is QK_K=256, matching K-quant conventions
- vec_dot_type is Q8_K, consistent with Q2_K–Q6_K family
- vec_dot functions are numerically consistent with dequantize (both apply identical QJL correction)
- Quantized types route through the existing scalar flash attention path via vec_dot — no attention kernel changes needed

## Block structures

| Type | Block size | Bytes | bpw | Layout |
|------|-----------|-------|-----|--------|
| pq4_0 | 256 | 66 | 2.0625 | `d[2] + qs[64]` (128 × 4-bit angles) |
| pq3_0 | 256 | 82 | 2.5625 | `d[2] + qs[48]` (128 × 3-bit) + `qj[32]` (256 sign bits) |
| pq2_0 | 256 | 66 | 2.0625 | `d[2] + qs[32]` (128 × 2-bit) + `qj[32]` (256 sign bits) |

## Files changed

- `ggml/include/ggml.h` — enum values 41-43, COUNT=44
- `ggml/src/ggml-common.h` — block struct definitions
- `ggml/src/ggml-quants.{h,c}` — quantize/dequantize implementations
- `ggml/src/ggml.c` — type_traits + quantize_chunk cases
- `ggml/src/ggml-cpu/ggml-cpu.c` — CPU type_traits (from_float, vec_dot)
- `ggml/src/ggml-cpu/quants.{h,c}` — vec_dot + quantize_row implementations
- `ggml/src/ggml-cpu/arch-fallback.h` — generic fallback macros
- `ggml/src/ggml-cpu/arch/{arm,x86,riscv}/quants.c` — forwarding stubs
- `common/arg.cpp` — KV cache type whitelist

## Test plan

- [x] Compiles cleanly on macOS ARM64 (Apple Silicon)
- [ ] Perplexity regression: `llama-perplexity -m <model> --cache-type-k pq3_0 --cache-type-v pq3_0`
- [ ] Long-context eval: Needle-in-a-Haystack at 32K/64K with pq3_0 vs q4_0 vs f16
- [ ] Memory measurement: peak RSS at various context lengths
- [ ] Round-trip quantize→dequantize MSE vs f16 baseline
- [ ] Cross-platform: x86_64 (AVX2), ARM64 (NEON)

## References

- [TurboQuant: Redefining AI Efficiency with Extreme Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.19874) — Zandieh et al., ICLR 2026
- [Quantized Johnson-Lindenstrauss](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.03482) — Zandieh et al., AAAI 2025
- [PolarQuant](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.02617) — Zandieh et al., AISTATS 2026
